coredata.dc:title: High fluorescent nitrogen − doped carbon dots derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola for detecting tetracyclines in aquaculture water and rat serum samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingmin
An, Jing
Zhang, Zhengqi
Zhu, Haimei
Liang, Xiao
Yang, Song
Sheng, Kangliang
Chen, Liping
Lu, Hengqian
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a kind of broad − spectrum and effective antibiotics that have been widely used in veterinary and human medicine, which may pose serious risk to human health if they are accumulated in human body. The fluorescence method using nanomaterials as probes has been gradually used for the detection of TCs because of its simple operation, high sensitivity and recognition ability. Herein, a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N − CDs) derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The N − CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, high quantum yield (QY) of 60.99 %, and excellent photostability, which was employed as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of TCs, including oxytetracycline (OXY), doxycycline (DOX), tetracycline (TCY) and minocycline (MNO). The fluorescence intensity of the N − CDs decreased linearly with the increasing concentration of OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO, and their concentrations were in the range of 0.6 – 73.3 μM, 0 – 95.2 μM, 6.5 – 72.3 μM and 10 – 52.6 μM, respectively. As for practical application, the as − prepared N − CDs could not only be effectively applied for determination of TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum samples with low relative standard deviation (RSD) and good recoveries, but also track the concentration change of TCs in rats blood model over time. Towards the fluorescence detection mechanism, the results of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes proved that the fluorescent quenching of N − CDs occurred from internal filtration effect (IFE) in the presence of TCs. This work provides guidance for the development of new TCs sensors based on fluorescent nanomaterials, and has a good application prospect in the quality control of agriculture, food and pharmaceutical production processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Sanghuangporus lonicericola
Tetracyclines detection
Fluorescent probe
Aquaculture water
Rat serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of antibiotics by the N − CDs
Detections of TCs in real samples by the N − CDs
Detections of TCs in serum of rats by the N − CDs
Optical properties of the N − CDs
Detection of antibiotics in aqueous solutions by the N − CDs
Detection of antibiotics in real samples by the N − CDs
Possible mechanism of the detection to TCs by the N − CDs

Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China
State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218), the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University (JM Wang) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang). All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis of N − CDs derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola and its application in TCs detection. (b) Typical TEM image (inset: HRTEM image) and (c) particle size distribution of the N − CDs.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FT − IR spectrum, (c) XPS survey spectrum, (d) high − resolution C 1 s, (e) high − resolution N 1 s and (f) high − resolution O 1 s spectra of the N − CDs.

(a) UV − vis and PL emission spectra (λ ex = 370 nm) of N − CDs dispersed in aqueous medium (Inset: the corresponding photographs of the CDs under UV lamp (365 nm)). PL emission spectra of the N − CDs (b) under different excitation wavelengths and (c) different concentrations. Relative fluorescence intensity of N − CDs at (d) different pH (1 ∼ 12), (e) different temperature (0 ℃, 4 ℃, 16 ℃ and 37 ℃), (f) different acid ion. (λex = 370 nm, CN−CDs = 0.1 mg ml−1).

(a) Fluorescence images of N − CD aqueous solution with various antibiotics under UV irradiation (365 nm). (b) Relative fluorescence intensity of N − CDs in aqueous solution when different organic molecules were introduced. (λ ex = 370 nm, CN−CDs = 0.1 mg ml−1).

Fluorescence emission spectra of N − CDs aqueous solution upon the addition of various concentration of (a) OXY, (c) DOX, (e) TCY and (g) MNO. The relationship between F0/F and concentration of (b) OXY, (d) DOX, (f) TCY and (h) MNO. (λ ex = 370 nm, CN−CDs = 0.1 mg ml−1).

(a) UV–vis spectra of TCY and N–CDs in the absence and presence of TCY; Fluorescent excitation (orange dotted line) and emission spectra (orange solid line) of N–CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetime experiments of N–CDs in the absence and presence of TCY.
Determination of four TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum samples with standard addition method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: High fluorescent nitrogen − doped carbon dots derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola for detecting tetracyclines in aquaculture water and rat serum samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a kind of broad − spectrum and effective antibiotics that have been widely used in veterinary and human medicine, which may pose serious risk to human health if they are accumulated in human body. The fluorescence method using nanomaterials as probes has been gradually used for the detection of TCs because of its simple operation, high sensitivity and recognition ability. Herein, a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N − CDs) derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The N − CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, high quantum yield (QY) of 60.99 %, and excellent photostability, which was employed as a fluorescent probe for selective detection of TCs, including oxytetracycline (OXY), doxycycline (DOX), tetracycline (TCY) and minocycline (MNO). The fluorescence intensity of the N − CDs decreased linearly with the increasing concentration of OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO, and their concentrations were in the range of 0.6 – 73.3 μM, 0 – 95.2 μM, 6.5 – 72.3 μM and 10 – 52.6 μM, respectively. As for practical application, the as − prepared N − CDs could not only be effectively applied for determination of TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum samples with low relative standard deviation (RSD) and good recoveries, but also track the concentration change of TCs in rats blood model over time. Towards the fluorescence detection mechanism, the results of UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes proved that the fluorescent quenching of N − CDs occurred from internal filtration effect (IFE) in the presence of TCs. This work provides guidance for the development of new TCs sensors based on fluorescent nanomaterials, and has a good application prospect in the quality control of agriculture, food and pharmaceutical production processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), as a member of versatile broad − spectrum antibiotics, have been extensively applied in animal husbandry, aquaculture, and breeding industry benefits of good antimicrobial effectiveness and low cost [1–3]. However, it has become a consensus that the abuse and difficult degradation of TCs will inevitably have an impact on food chains, soils, natural water systems, and other aquatic environments [4]. More seriously, environmental pollution allows TCs to enter human body easily, threating human health [5,6]. Therefore, it is of great necessity to develop a simple, rapid and effective method to detect residual TCs in the environment and human body. So far, a variety of strategies have been adopted to detect TCs, such as liquid chromatography [7], surface enhanced Raman scattering [8], antimicrobial screening [9], enzyme − linked aptamer assay [10], etc. Generally, those techniques present high sensitivity and identification ability for target analytes, but require complicated sample pretreatment procedures, sophisticated and expensive instruments and expert technical skill, hindering their application in routine analysis.

To circumvent these drawbacks, fluorescence − based detection methods with operational convenience, low cost, real − time detection and high sensitivity, have raised global concerns as an alternative strategy for detection and determination of TCs [11–13]. In the past decades, various fluorescent nanomaterials had been designed to construct fluorescence sensors. Yang et al. have built an enhanced − fluorescence system by DNA − templated gold nanoclusters for simple, sensitive and cost − effective quantification of TCs in human urine and milk samples [14]. This enhanced − fluorescence system permitted available detection of TCs in a linear range of 0.01–5 μM with a detection limit of 4 nM at a signal − to − noise ratio of 3. Imani − Nabiyyi et al. have established a chemiluminescence system based on cysteine − modified CdTe quantum dots (QDs) to measure tetracycline and oxytetracycline in water and honey samples [15]. The QDs can catalyze the reaction of tetracycline and oxytetracycline with sodium periodate to produce reactive oxygen species under alkaline conditions, which can stimulate the generation of chemiluminescence. Li et al. have reported an amino − functionalized Al − organic frameworks for fluorescent detection of TCs in milk with recoveries of 85.15–112.13 % [16]. The detection result was highly consistent with that of HPLC (P > 0.05), exhibiting good food detection applicability. Nonetheless, heavy metal nanoclusters are limited by the low stability and high cost, the disadvantages of semiconductor QDs are high toxicity and poor biocompatibility, and metal − organic frameworks exist the problem of complex synthesis and difficult removal of organic solvents, which may not meet the practical application needs of detecting TCs in the environment or human body [2]. Thus, it is promising to develop fluorescent nanomaterials that can effectively remedy these disadvantages and build classical TCs fluorescence sensors.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new star of fluorescent nanomaterials, which have attracted extensive attention due to their highly attractive properties such as bright fluorescence, good dispersion, excellent thermal/photostability, non − toxicity or low toxicity and easy mass production [17]. Quasi − spherical nanodots (less than10 nm) with graphitic, crystalline or amorphous sp2 hybridized carbon cores and carbon dioxide surfaces were successfully synthesized using “top − down” and “bottom − up” approaches, which can be called CDs. In the bottom − up approach, the hydrothermal method is widely used, in which different carbon sources (especially natural products) are dispersed in water or organic solvents, placed in a closed hydrothermal kettle, and reacted at high temperature and pressure to prepare CDs [18,19]. Meanwhile, CDs have been shown to significantly improve their fluorescence properties by functionalizing with nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus and other heteroatoms. Among them, nitrogen doped CDs (N − CDs) are superior to other element doped CDs, because the position of nitrogen in the periodic table is close to carbon, making the lattice positions of the two match [20,21]. N − CDs with excellent optical properties can be obtained from organic molecule compounds using citrate based compounds (carbon sources) and ammonium based compounds (nitrogen sources) [19]. However, these chemicals are expensive and harmful to the environment, which is not conducive to their large − scale application. Therefore, hydrothermal synthesis of N − CDs using nitrogen − containing natural products as precursors is considered to be a green and sustainable nanotechnology. Synthetic N − CDs have been widely employed in biological imaging, cancer treatment, drug delivery, optoelectronic devices and fluorescent sensors [22–25]. Although many N − CDs synthesized from natural products have been reported for TCs detection, such as white berries [26], leek [27] and rose flowers [28], their practical feasibility is restricted by low fluorescence quantum yields and poor reproducibility of natural products. As a simple, economical and environmentally friendly carbon source, fungi rich in proteins, sugars, etc. are often used by researchers for the synthesis of N − CDs [29–33], in which polymerized and carbonized carbon skeleton acts as carbon core and heteroatoms (N, S, P, etc.) can improve the optical properties of CDs. Besides, using fungi as precursors, hydrothermal synthesis of doped CDs does not require any organic reagents, and the product has excellent performance [29–33]. Sanghuangporus lonicericola is a kind of very precious macrofungi with important medicinal value, which is abundant in Anhui province. So far, the research of Sanghuangporus lonicericola and its extract in biomedicine has been gradually deepened, but the research on its use as a carbon source to produce CDs for environmental detection has not been reported.

Herein, Sanghuangporus lonicericola was proposed as precursors for one − pot hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N − CDs). The N − CDs exhibited good water solubility, excellent photostability (pH 3–11 and temperature 0–70 °C), high nitrogen doping and fluorescence quantum yield. More importantly, the response of N − CDs to several different classes of antibiotics (tetracyclines, β − lactams, and aminoglycosides etc.) revealed that it could be specifically quenched by four TCs, namely oxytetracycline (OXY), doxycycline (DOX), tetracycline (TCY) and minocycline (MNO). Due to the internal filtering effect (IFE), the N − CDs based fluorescent sensor possess a wide linear range and low detection limit with the addition of TCs. Further, the practical application of N − CDs as a fluorescence sensing platform for detecting TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum was investigated, and the results showed that the N − CDs exhibited good accuracy and sensitivity to TCs in real samples. Moreover, DOX in the blood of rats could be detected by the N − CDs after 30 min of over administration. Overall, this work offers a new strategy for the highly sensitive and visible detection of TCs in real samples, and supplies a new material for the design of fluorescence sensors.

All used chemicals of the processes were commercially available without further purification. Glucose, Agar − agar, Chloramphenicol, Streptomycin sulfate, Kanamycin sulfate, Acid ions (e. g. Na3PO4, C5H7O5COOK, K2C2O4, CH3COONa, C10H14N2Na2O8, NaCl, Na2SO4) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), ethanol and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Amoxicillin (AMX), chloromycetin (CHL), cephalexin (LEX), penicillin potassium (PEN), ampicillin sodium (AMP), neomycin sulfate (NEO), streptomycin (STR), paromomycin sulfate (PAR), gentamicin (GEN), hygromycin (HYG) kanamycin (KAN), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTE), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (OXY), doxycycline hydrochloride (DOX), tetracycline hydrochloride (TCY) and minocycline hydrochloride (MNO) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin/streptomycin, and trypsin were purchased from Hyclone (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared in Double distilled water (ddH2O). Temperature during all other experiments was maintained at 25 °C, unless otherwise mentioned.

The morphology of samples were performed by the transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM − 2100). X − ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on an X’Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu − Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å) at a scanning rate of 4° min−1. The structural of samples were characterized by Fourier − transform infrared (FT − IR) and X − ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), where FTIR spectroscopy analyses were tested using a Vertex80 + Hyperion2000 spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) employing the KBr pellet method and XPS spectra were obtained by Thermo Electron ESCALAB 250Xi X − ray photoelectron spectrometer (New York, America). The Hitachi F − 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Horiba Fluoro max plus ultrafast time resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan) were used to examine fluorescence and fluorescence lifetime of samples, respectively. The quantum yields were measured by using HORIBA JOBIN YVON FLUORAMAX − 4P full function steady state and transient fluorescence spectrometer (Paris, France). UV/Vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Meperida UV − 2450 spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China).

Sanghuangporus lonicericola used in this experiment was preserved in the School of Life Sciences of Anhui University and sequenced into Lonicera microphylla. The specific culture process was listed as follows: Sanghuangporus lonicericola was inoculated into PDB medium, cultured at 28 ℃ and 180 rpm for 10 days, and then filtered with 6 layers of gauze. Centrifuging the filtered mycelium at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove the culture medium, then washing with water and ethanol for 3 times, respectively. After centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the mycelium of Sanghuangporus lonicericola, freeze − drying to obtain the dried mycelium of Sanghuangporus lonicericola for subsequent experiments.

The nitrogen doped carbon dots (N − CDs) derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 1 a). Briefly, 5 g of Sanghuangporus lonicericola powder with 30 mL of ddH2O were added into a 50 mL Teflon − lined stainless − steel autoclave. After heating at 200 ◦C for 24 h, the auto − clave was cooled to the room temperature. The obtained brown solution was filtrated through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Subsequently, the solution was dialyzed for 3 days using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500). Finally, the dialyzed solution was vacuum − dried at − 60 °C to collect a brown powder (N − CDs).

Before that, we prepared three batches of N − CDs derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola at different growth cycle (10 d, 15 d and 20 d, respectively) and evaluated their fluorescence properties. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the peak positions of the UV − vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation emission spectra of the three batches of N − CD were the same, but the fluorescence emission intensity decreased with the increase of the growth time of Sanghuangporus lonicericola. It is speculated that the different content of organic substances such as protein, flavonoids, glycopeptides and polysaccharides in the three batches of Sanghuangporus lonicericola lead to the difference in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the Sanghuangporus lonicericola cultured for 10 d was finally selected as the carbon source to prepare N − CDs.

pH stability: the HCl and NaOH solution were used to regulate solutions with pH values of 1–12. The mixed solutions were obtained by adding 300 μL N − CDs solution (1 mg ml−1) to 2700 μL of water with different pH as described above, and the effect of pH on N − CDs solution was obtained based on the fluorescence spectra (emitted at 450 nm) of mixed solution (the fluorescence intensity the pH of N − CDs solution at pH = 6 was used as a control). Three parallel experiments were set for each group.

Temperature stability: ddH2O was used to prepare 0.1 mg ml−1N − CDs solution, which was incubated in a water bath at 0, 4, 16, 37 and 70 ℃ for 30 min. And the fluorescence spectra (emitted at 450 nm) of N − CDs solution excited by 370 nm at different temperatures were measured. The relative fluorescence intensity was calculated by taking the fluorescence intensity at room temperature (25 ℃) as the control. Three parallel experiments were set for each group.

Acid ion stability: 0.1 M of Na3PO4, C5H7O5COOK, K2C2O4, CH3COONa, C10H14N2Na2O8, NaCl, Na2SO4 and 2 mg ml−1 BSA solutions were configured. And then, 100 μL of the above solution was added to 2900 μL of 0.1 mg ml−1N − CDs solution to obtain a mixed solution, and fluorescence spectra (emitted at 450 nm) excited by 370 nm were measured to analysis the effect of acid ions on N − CDs solution. Three parallel experiments were set up for each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 mg ml−1N − CDs solution and 0.1 mM various antibiotics was configured into a solution with a total volume of 3 mL. The fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were obtained to evaluate the response of the N − CDs solution to various antibiotics. Three parallel experiments were set up for each group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of TCs in real samples by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Aquaculture water: the wastewater was taken from the fish pond in front of the experimental building (School of Life Sciences, Anhui University). Centrifuging at 12000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the supernatant, which was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane for three times, and the filtrate was placed in a reagent bottle and stored at 4 ℃.

Rat serum: blood was take from rats' orbit by blood capillary, and then placed in the blood collection vessel. After adding 1.5 times volume of acetonitrile, blood was placed in an environment of 18 ℃ to vibrate for 2 h. Finally, the blood was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, and diluted 20 times with ddH2O. The serum was stored in a reagent bottle at − 20 ℃ [34].
Further, the detection sensitivity of the N − CDs to TCs solutions with different concentrations in real samples were evaluated by fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of TCs in serum of rats by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experimental procedures completely adhered to the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Anhui University, China and were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Anhui University, China.

Eight − week − old male SD mice were kept under specific pathogen free conditions, allowed free access to drinking water and sterilized standard diet under the standard 12 h light/dark cycle. After acclimation for 7 days, the rats were weighed, and administered by DOX (30 mg kg−1). After 5, 10, 20 and 30 min of administration, blood was take from rats' orbit by blood capillary, and then the serum samples containing DOX were obtained by the treatment method of serum in 2.6. The content of DOX in the serum was detected by fluorescence method based on the N − CDs.

TEM was employed to directly observe the morphology and size distributions of N − CDs, as shown in Fig. 1b, there was no aggregations occurring in the N − CDs, demonstrating their favorable dispersity. Meanwhile, the N − CDs were spherical with an average size of 2.8 nm and the majority size distribution range from 1.93 nm to 4.03 nm (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 1b depicted that the lattice spacing of N − CDs was 0.21 nm, which was in accordance with the plane (100) of graphite carbon [35].

Further, the crystallinity, functionalization, surface structure and chemical composition of the N − CDs were studied by XRD pattern, FT − IR and XPS spectra. The XRD pattern of the N − CDs in Fig. 2 a displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 23° and a small diffraction peak around 42°, which were attributed to the graphite (002) and (100) plane, respectively, matching well with the HRTEM results [18,36]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the broad peak in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 was ascribed to O H and N H stretching vibrations, while the wide peaks in the area of 2900–2940 cm−1 belonged to CH stretching vibrations [37]. The distinguishing peaks of 1657 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 corresponded to the OC–NH/CN stretching, the absorption peak of 1068 cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretching vibrations [38]. Moreover, the XPS survey spectrum of the N − CDs (Fig. 2c) present three characteristic peaks at 288 eV, 401 eV and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [37,38], where the atomic content of C, N and O was 60.6 %, 9.91 % and 28.8 %, respectively. It is noteworthy that the atom content of N (9.91 %) is comparable to other biomass − based doped CDs reported in the literature[13,31,32], which is beneficial to the improvement of fluorescence properties. The total atomic ratio of CDs was less than 100 %, probably because Sanghuangporus lonicericola as carbon source may contain other elements (such as P, S).

The high − resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be split into four main peaks of CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, at 284.7、285.6、286.6 and 288.4 eV, respectively [39]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) was divided into three characteristic peaks at 399.3, 399.9 and 401.3 eV, indicating the existence of pyridinic–N, pyrrolic–N, and graphitic–N, respectively [40–42]. More importantly, the configurations of C N in CDs can affect the PL intensity substantially, due to the effect of delocalized π electrons from pyridinic − N [42–44]. In addition, the homologous O1s spectrum were shown in Fig. 2f, in which three typical peaks can be attributed to O–CO (531.7 eV), CO (532.6 eV) and CO (533.2 eV), respectively [19,45,46]. The above results demonstrated the presence of hydrophilic groups such as − OH, −COOH, −NH2 and O–CO bonds on the surface of the N − CDs, which equipped the N − CDs with good water solubility for different applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It can be seen from the inset in Fig. 3 a that the as − synthesized N − CDs possess good dispersion in aqueous solution and the solution remains transparent, pale yellow and transparent under visible light, while emerges bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, which is consistent with the results of Fig. 2. UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra and quantum yield (QY) were employed to further study the optical properties of the N − CDs. As represented in Fig. 3a, there is an obvious absorption peaks at 240 nm in the UV–vis spectrum, which may be assigned to the π → π* transition of the sp2 domain. The noticeable peak centered at 353 nm could be attributed to the n → π* transition of CO and NH groups [38]. Meanwhile, the N − CDs exhibit a distinct PL emission at 450 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Fig. 3b performed the fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths, it can be observed that the N − CDs have a certain excitation − dependence and the optimal excitation wavelength is 370 nm. The absolute QY was recorded and shown in Fig. S2, the N − CDs own a high QY value of 60.99 %, which is comparable to that of the other fungal − derived CDs on the basis of the same aqueous solution (Table S1) [29–33]. In addition, the concentration dependence of the N − CDs were tested in Fig. 3c, the results proved that the fluorescence intensity of N − CDs become the strongest at 0.1 mg ml−1. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 370 nm and the concentration of 0.1 mg ml−1 of the N − CDs were selected for detection applications.

The effects of pH, temperature, acid ion, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and preservation time were chosen to evaluate the stability of these N − CDs as fluorescent probes in practical applications [47,48]. The pH range of 1–12 covered most environmental temperature. It can be seen from Fig. 3d and S3a that the fluorescence intensity of N − CDs solution changes slightly in the pH range of pH 3–11, while that was weakened in strong acid (pH = 1, 2) and strong base (pH = 12) environments, especially in the pH = 1. Thus, the N − CDs have good stability in a wide pH range environment (pH = 3 ∼ 11). Meanwhile, ice − water mixture (0 ℃), low temperature (4 ℃), room temperature (16, 37 ℃) and high temperature (70 ℃) were selected to explore the effect of temperature on the fluorescence of N − CDs (Fig. 3e, S3b). It is found that the fluorescence intensity of N − CDs changes slightly at different temperatures, indicating that the N − CDs can be used in special environments and high temperature environments all year round. Considering that many CDs can be utilized for detection in vivo, the influence of anions (Cl−, C6H5O7 3−, CH3COO−, C10H14N2O8 2−, C2O4 2−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−) and BSA in the microenvironment on the fluorescence properties of N − CDs were explored and shown in Fig. 3f. After adding various acid ions as well as BSA, the fluorescence intensity of N − CDs solution was relatively stable, and the effect of acid ions on the fluorescence of N − CDs could be neglected. Additionally, Fig. S3c displayed PL stability of the N–CDs solution under different storage time, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly within 7 days, and decreased nearly half at 24 days. Moreover, the absolute QY was tested as 34.04 % after keeping the N − CDs solution for 30 days, which was still comparable to other materials (Table S1)[23–27]. The above results prove that the N − CDs have good photostability and can be applied in a variety of complex environments, which lays the foundation for detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics in aqueous solutions by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As it can be obviously observed from Fig. 4 a that the addition of tetracyclines (TCs) carried considerably fluorescence decreasing of N − CDs, compared with the fluorescence images of N − CDs with different antibiotics (β − lactam antibiotics, aminoglycoside antibiotics) under UV irradiation (λ = 365 nm). The selectivity of the N − CDs to TCs was investigated by comparing the relative fluorescence intensity of N − CDs before and after the addition of different types of antibiotics (CHL, PEN, AMP etc.). As displayed in Fig. 4b, only five TCs (CTE, OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO) could decrease the fluorescence intensity of N − CDs, while the decreased effect of other antibiotics was almost negligible, convincing that N − CDs can be an effective fluorescence sensor for TCs. Due to the similar chemical structure of TCs, the N − CDs can selectively response to these TCs [31]. However, the N − CDs have a poor selection effect on CTE, which is one of TCs. According to the chemical structure of TCs in Fig. S4, it is suspected that the chlorine atom in the chemical structure reduces the density of electron cloud on benzene ring, so that CTE is more difficult to quench the N − CDs than other TCs.

Four TCs (OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO) were choose to verify the sensitivity of the N − CDs to TCs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescent intensity of N − CDs at 450 nm decreased with increasing the concentration of OXY from 0 to 250 μM, while the same decreased with the increasing concentrations of DOX, TCY and MNO (Fig. 5c, 5e and 5 g), respectively. Fig. 5b depicted the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of N − CDs and the concentration of added OXY. There is a linear relationship between F0 /F and the added OXY concentration in the range of 0.6–73.3 µM (Fig. S5a) and the linear equation can be calculated as y = 0.01457 × + 1.0015 with the correlation coefficient as R2 = 0.99935, while the limit of detection (LOD) was tested as 3 nM. Similarly, Fig. 5d, 5f and 5 h show the relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of the N − CDs and the concentrations of DOX, TCY and MNO, respectively. Further, Fig. S5b show that there is a linear relationship between DOX and the F0 /F of N − CDs in the concentration range of 0–95.2 µM with a calibration curve as y = 0.01587 × + 1.0004 (R2 = 0.99736, LOD = 3 nM). Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S5c and S5d, the linear relationships between the relative fluorescence intensity of N − CDs and the concentrations of TCY (6.5–72.3 µM) and MNO (10–52.6 µM) were y = 0.00999x + 1.1063 (R2 = 0.99746, LOD = 6.6 nM) and y = 0.01904x + 0.91105 (R2 = 0.99213, LOD = 13.3 nM), respectively. The comparison between various fluorescence sensors for TCs detection in different samples has been listed in Table S2. It can be seen that N − CDs have a linear range and LOD comparable to other reports [14,16,37,49–51,].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics in real samples by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the practical detection value of the N − CDs for TCs, different concentrations of the four TCs were added into the aquaculture water and rat serum, and the real samples were measured by fluorescence spectra to determine the concentrations of TCs. The calculation was based on the previously obtained calibration curve. As listed in Table 1 , the recoveries for detecting TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum ranged from 90.37 to 111.7 % with relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) less than 6.5 %, which is comparable with some carbon dots used for drug detection[30], proving the reliable performance of N − CD in detecting TCs in real samples. The above results show that the N − CDs can be used as a selective and eco − friendly sensor for the detection of TCs, and have a great application prospect in environmental monitoring.

Furthermore, the N − CDs were used to detect the content of DOX in the bloods of rats after excessive administration of DOX (30 mg kg−1) for a certain period of time. After 5, 10, 20 and 30 min of administration, the bloods of rats were taken from the eyes and the amount of DOX in the serum was detected by fluorescence analysis. The results showed that DOX in rat serum could not be detected by N − CDs at 5, 10 and 20 min, but 30 min. Fig. S6 present the fluorescence spectra of DOX in serum detected by N − CDs, and the standard curve calculated as y = 0.01648 × + 1.04693 (R2 = 0.99063). According to the curve, it can be calculated that the content of DOX in the rats’ serum of the two groups of rats was similar after 30 min, in which the DOX content of group 1 was 0.699 μM (RSD = 0.23 %) and group 2 was 0.784 μM (RSD = 0.47 %). Therefore, the above results prove that using the N − CDs as the fluorescence sensor for detecting TCs exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity, and the fluorescence method test is feasible for quickly and accurately detecting the TCs in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the detection to TCs by the N − CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO belong to TCs with similar structures, and TCY is one of the four antibiotics with poor response to N − CDs, TCY was selected as the representative of TCs to explore the mechanism of causing N − CD fluorescence quenching. PL, UV − vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime were consequently investigated. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), light induced electron transfer (PET) and internal filter effect (IFE) are generally considered as the main fluorescence quenching mechanisms of quenchers on nano − sensors[16]. The UV − vis spectra (Fig. 6 a) diaplayed that the absorption spectrum of TCY solution possesses a negligible spectral overlap with the emission spectrum of N − CDs, so the FRET mechanism is excluded. However, the strong absorption of TCY effectively inhibited the excitation energy absorption of N − CDs excited by 370 nm, which results in a low populated emissive state of N − CDs, thus significantly quenching the fluorescence of the sensor. The IFE can be estimated according to eq (1) [16]:
(1)
F
correct
F
=
2.3 × d ×
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
×
2.3 × s ×
A
em
×
10
g
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

Where F is the maximum FL intensity measured by spectrophotometer, Fcorrect is the FL intensity corrected by removing IFE from F, Aex and Aem are the absorbance of N − CD added with TCY at 370 and 450 nm, respectively, g is the distance between the edge of the cuvette and the edge of the excitation beam (0.4 cm), s is the thickness of the excitation beam (0.1 cm) and d is the width of the cuvette (1.0 cm). The calculation results were shown in Table S3, and the quenching rate after correction is close to the actual measured, indicating that IFE is the main quenching mechanism.

In addition, the mechanism of carbon dots based fluorescence sensing, which almost includes static quenching or dynamic quenching, has also been discussed [37,38]. The fluorescence intensity of N–CDs gradually decreased with the increase of TCs concentration, and the fluorescence quenching was fitted by Stern–Volmer equation, as shown in equation (2):
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
(
R
2
= 0.99746 )

Where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity emitted by 370 nm in the absence and presence of TCs, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of TCs, and K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. The K sv was assigned a value of 9.99 × 103 M−1 here, ranging from 6.5 − 72.3 µM for a linear regression equation (Fig. S5c). Since K qτ0 = K sv, where τ0 can be measured as 3.99669 ns. Thus, the double molecular quenching constant K q can be calculated as 2.5 × 1012 M−1s−1, the large value of K q may be due to static quenching, that is, the formation of non–fluorescent complexes when the quenching agent and fluorescent material molecules combined in the ground state[52,53].

The lifetimes of N − CDs in the absence and presence of TCY were measured, which is a useful method for distinguishing dynamic quenching from static quenching [52,53]. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence lifetimes of the N − CDs and N − CDs + TCY were determined to be 3.99669 ns and 3.87919 ns, individually, the τ/τ 0 value tended to 1, confirming that the fluorescence quenching caused by TCs was probably static quenching [37,38,53].

In summary, a new N − doped carbon dots (N − CDs) derived from Sanghuangporus lonicericola was first synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. The as − synthesized N − CDs exhibited favorable solubility, high quantum yields (60.99 %) and good photostability, which can be used in environments at pH range of 3–11 and temperature range of 0–70 ℃. More importantly, the N − CDs can act as a fluorescent probe for TCs detection, including OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO with high selectivity and sensitivity. In detail, the fluorescence intensity of the N − CDs decreased linearly with increasing the concentration of OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO in range of 0.6 – 73.3 μM, 0 – 95.2 μM, 6.5 – 72.3 μM and 12 – 52.6 μM, respectively. And the LOD of OXY, DOX, TCY and MNO were 3, 3, 6.6 and 13.3 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescent nano − sensors have been successfully used to detect TCs in aquaculture water and rat serum with high recovery rate, and also showed high sensitivity in DOX detection of real rats’ blood. Due to internal filtration effect (IFE), the fluorescence of N − CDs was quenched with the addition of TCs, which built the foundation of TCs quantification. This work provides a sensitive, rapid, visible and on − site analysis platform for detecting TCs in wastewater and human body, which has important practical significance.

Jingmin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jing An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhengqi Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Haimei Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiao Liang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Song Yang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Kangliang Sheng: Validation, Formal analysis: Liping Chen: Validation, Formal analysis. Hengqian Lu: Validation, Formal analysis, Yongzhong Wang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218), the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University (JM Wang) and the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang). All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108517.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
